trajectory_summary: The call opens on a complaint and closes on a booking, which is a net positive movement — but the foundation is fragile. Blair booked to stop the calls, not because curiosity was created. The broken-promise injury from the prior rep was never specifically acknowledged, and Blair's 'I should be free' hedge at 01:37 signals conditionality. The appointment exists; its durability is uncertain.
outcome_dispute: v1 outcome tag is unknown. Based on the transcript, the call ends with a booked appointment (Monday at 10AM). However, the booking is conditional — Blair said 'I should be free' and 'might as well,' both of which are compliance signals rather than commitment signals. The correct tag is tentative_appointment rather than confirmed_appointment, if that distinction exists in Ion's system.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Button-up completeness — converting a complaint-booking into a durable appointment
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I can I can get that all all scheduled out for you? That way the dialer doesn't keep calling you.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Matt booked the appointment but delivered none of the button_up required elements: no one-hour block, no phone-appointment specification, no no-driving/at-computer anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake. On a call where Blair's only stated motivation is stopping the calls, the handshake and the appointment framing are the only tools that convert compliance into commitment. This is a repeatable 90-second fix with direct impact on show rate.

key_moments.quote: I spoke to someone. I told him, maybe something next week, but he was gonna call back. And now I've had five more people call me.
key_moments.stewart_read: Blair is disclosing a specific broken promise — a prior rep made a commitment and the system violated it five more times. This is a trust injury, not just a contact-volume complaint. Matt's sequencing choice (close first, repair second) is defensible: he moved to booking immediately at 00:55 and offered the apology after the appointment was locked at 01:56. The strategic read holds — don't let the complaint derail the booking. But the post-booking repair ('sorry for all the phone calls, I know our dialer is kind of aggressive') is too generic. It addresses the volume but not the broken promise. Blair said someone told them they'd call back; Matt never acknowledged that specific injury.

key_moments.quote: Oh, okay. I can I can get that all all scheduled out for you? That way the dialer doesn't keep calling you.
key_moments.stewart_read: Matt's booking pitch is framed entirely as a dialer-stopper, not as a value proposition. The appointment is being offered as relief from harassment. This means Blair's reason to show up is 'calls will stop' — which evaporates the moment the calls stop, whether or not Blair attends. None of the button_up required elements are present: no one-hour block stated, no phone-appointment specification, no no-driving/at-computer anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake. The booking is structurally incomplete.

key_moments.quote: I'll get that all taken care of for you.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the one genuine positive signal in the call. Matt makes a personal accountability pledge — not 'the system will stop calling' but 'I will take care of it.' On a customer whose trust has been damaged by a broken institutional promise, a personal commitment is the right currency. This is a micro_trade: Matt is substituting his personal accountability for the impersonal dialer that caused the injury. It is brief and not amplified, but it is the correct instinct.

key_moments.quote: Maybe ten. I should be free.
key_moments.stewart_read: Blair's 'I should be free' is a second explicit conditionality marker, following 'might as well' at 01:29. Blair is not confirming availability — they are hedging. A committed customer says 'ten works' or 'I'll be home.' 'I should be free' leaves the door open for a no-show without guilt. Matt confirmed the time without probing the hedge or anchoring the commitment.
